6 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

If you’ve been working with Flutter for some time, chances are you’ve heard senior developers casually mention terms like CanvasKit, Wasm, Skia while discussing Flutter Web performance.
At first, these terms can sound intimidating.
Questions such as:

• What is canvasKit and why do we need it?

• What is WebAssembly?

• Are they related?

• What exactly does Skia do?

• Why they are needed for web and not for android/iOS?
are extremely common.
Let’s break down Flutter’s rendering architecture from first principles.

The Big Picture: How Flutter Renders UI
Suppose you write the following Flutter widget:
Container(
width: 100,
height: 100,
color: Colors.red,
)Flutter cannot directly display this widget on the screen.
Instead, rendering happens in multiple stages:
Flutter Widgets
↓
Flutter Framework
↓
Flutter Engine
↓
Graphics Engine (Skia/Impeller)
↓
Platform Graphics API (OpenGL,Vulkan,Metal)
↓
GPU
↓
ScreenEvery layer has a specific responsibility.

What Exactly is Skia?
Skia is Google’s 2D graphics engine written in C++.
Flutter uses Skia to render almost everything:

• Text

• Shapes

• Shadows

• Images

• Animations

• CustomPaint widgets
Skia is not unique to Flutter.
It is also used in:

• Google Chrome

• Android

• ChromeOS

• Firefox (partially)

• Android System UI
Think of Skia as a highly optimized graphics library.
When Flutter wants to display something, it eventually asks Skia to draw it.
Conceptually:
Flutter:
Text("Hello")
↓
Skia:
Draw text "Hello"
Font size: 20
Position: (100, 50)
Does Skia Actually Draw Pixels?
The answer is yes, but not always directly.
Skia supports two rendering modes:
1. Software Rendering (CPU)
In software rendering, Skia computes every pixel itself.
Conceptually:
For every pixel inside rectangle:
Set color = RedThis is CPU-intensive and generally slower.
2. GPU Rendering (Most Common)
Modern Flutter applications usually use GPU rendering.
In this mode, Skia does not manually calculate every pixel.
Instead, Skia generates drawing commands and sends them to the GPU.
For example:
Draw rectangle
Position: x=10, y=20
Width: 100
Height: 100
Color: RedThe GPU then performs the actual rasterization.
This is significantly faster because GPUs are specifically designed for parallel graphics processing.

Then What Does the GPU Actually Do?
GPUs are specialized processors optimized for rendering millions of pixels simultaneously.
Unlike CPUs, GPUs can process many graphics operations in parallel.
Responsibilities of the GPU include:

• Rasterizing shapes

• Applying gradients

• Rendering shadows

• Running shaders

• Scaling images

• Drawing textures

• Compositing layers
Without GPUs, modern UIs and animations would be much slower.

What are OpenGL, Vulkan, Metal, and DirectX?
One common misconception is that applications communicate directly with the GPU.
They don’t.
Applications use graphics APIs.
Examples include:
Platform Graphics API
---------------------------------------------
Android OpenGL ES,Vulkan
iOS/macOS Metal
Windows DirectX
Linux OpenGL, VulkanThese APIs act as translators between software and hardware.
The rendering pipeline on Android/iOS/Windows looks like:
Android iOS Windows
------------------------------------------------------
Flutter Flutter Flutter
↓ ↓ ↓
Skia Skia Skia
↓ ↓ ↓
Vulkan/OpenGL Metal DirectX/OpenGL
↓ ↓ ↓
GPU GPU GPUYou can think of these APIs as drivers or communication protocols that allow software to instruct the GPU.

How Does Flutter Render on Mobile and Desktop?
Traditionally, Flutter uses native Skia libraries.
Flutter Framework
↓
Skia (native C++)
↓
OpenGL/Vulkan/Metal/DirectX
↓
GPUSince Android, iOS, Windows, Linux, and macOS can run native C++ code, Flutter simply ships Skia as a native library.

What Changes on the Web?
This is where many developers get confused.
Browsers cannot execute native C++ code directly.
For security and portability reasons, browsers can primarily execute:

• JavaScript

• WebAssembly (Wasm)
Since Skia is written in C++, it cannot run inside browsers as-is.
Therefore Flutter compiles Skia into WebAssembly.
Skia (C++)
↓
Compiled to WebAssembly
↓
Executed by BrowserThis browser version of Skia is known as CanvasKit.

Is CanvasKit a Separate Rendering Engine?
No.
CanvasKit is not a separate rendering engine.
CanvasKit is essentially:
Skia + WebAssembly + Browser BindingsIn other words:
CanvasKit is Skia running inside the browser.
This is why Flutter applications rendered using CanvasKit look very similar across Android, iOS, desktop, and web.

How browser communicates with GPU?
On native platforms:
Flutter
↓
Skia
↓
Vulkan/OpenGL/Metal
↓
GPUOn the web:
Flutter
↓
CanvasKit (Skia compiled to Wasm as browser can't run c++ version of skia)
↓
WebGL/WebGPU
↓
GPUThe browser exposes graphics capabilities through APIs such as:

• WebGL

• WebGPU
CanvasKit ultimately uses these browser APIs to access the GPU.
Therefore Flutter Web still benefits from hardware acceleration.

What Exactly is WebAssembly (Wasm)?
WebAssembly is a low-level binary instruction format designed for the web.
It allows languages such as:

• C++

• Rust

• C#

• Dart
to run efficiently inside browsers.
Think of Wasm as:
A portable, near-native execution format supported by modern browsers.
Browsers contain runtimes capable of executing both JavaScript and WebAssembly.

Is canvasKit is just skia compiled to wasm or something else?
CanvasKit is more than just the .wasm file, but the Wasm code is the core part of CanvasKit.
A more accurate definition is:
CanvasKit = Skia compiled to Wasm + JavaScript bindings/API + browser integration codeLet’s break it down.
If you start with native Skia:
Skia (C++ source code)When building CanvasKit, Google compiles Skia using Emscripten:
Skia (C++)
↓
Emscripten
↓
canvaskit.wasmThis produces the WebAssembly binary.
However, Flutter cannot directly call functions inside a .wasm file. Some glue code is needed.
So CanvasKit also includes JavaScript wrappers:
CanvasKit.MakeCanvasSurface(...)
CanvasKit.Color(...)
CanvasKit.Paint(...)These wrappers:

• load the .wasm file,

• expose convenient APIs,

• connect Skia with browser features like <canvas> and WebGL.
So the actual CanvasKit package looks conceptually like:
CanvasKit
│
├── canvaskit.wasm ← compiled Skia engine
├── canvaskit.js ← JS glue/bindings
└── browser integration ← canvas, WebGL setup, etc.Therefore:

• Saying “CanvasKit is Skia compiled to Wasm” is mostly correct in everyday Flutter discussions.

• Technically, the Wasm file itself is only one part of CanvasKit.

• The term CanvasKit usually refers to the entire web package that allows Skia to run inside the browser.
An analogy:
Skia (C++) = car engine
canvaskit.wasm = engine installed in a car
canvaskit.js + browser bindings = steering wheel, pedals, dashboard
Entire car = CanvasKitSo if someone says:
“Flutter Web uses CanvasKit”
they mean:
“Flutter Web uses the CanvasKit package, whose core is Skia compiled to WebAssembly.”
And if you inspect a Flutter web build using CanvasKit, you’ll typically see files similar to:
canvaskit/
├── canvaskit.js
└── canvaskit.wasmwhere canvaskit.wasm contains the actual compiled Skia engine.

How Did Flutter Web Originally Work?
Historically, Flutter Web used the following architecture:
Flutter App (Dart) Skia
↓ ↓
Compiled to JavaScript Compiled to WebAssemblyAt runtime:
JavaScript
↓
Calls CanvasKit APIs
↓
CanvasKit (Wasm)
↓
WebGL
↓
GPUNotice something important:
Skia never executes Flutter code.
Flutter code and Skia are independent components.
Flutter simply sends drawing instructions to Skia.

Can Flutter Code Also Be Compiled to Wasm?
Yes.
Modern Flutter versions support:
flutter build web --wasmIn this architecture:
Flutter App (Dart) Skia
↓ ↓
Compiled to WebAssembly Compiled to WebAssemblyBoth application code and rendering code run as Wasm modules.

Does Compiling Flutter to Wasm Improve Performance?
Potentially, yes.
Benefits may include:

• Faster CPU-intensive computations

• Reduced JavaScript ↔ Wasm communication overhead

• Better support for multithreading

• Improved rendering efficiency on modern browsers
However, for typical CRUD applications, users may not notice dramatic differences.
Performance improvements become more noticeable in:

• Graphics-heavy applications

• Large applications

• Complex animations

• Image processing

• Data-intensive workloads

Final Thoughts
Whenever you hear someone say:
“Flutter Web uses CanvasKit and Wasm”
what they really mean is:
Flutter UI
↓
Skia compiled to
↓
WebAssembly
↓
Browser Graphics APIs
↓
GPUThe key takeaway is:

• Skia is Flutter’s graphics engine.

• CanvasKit is Skia compiled for the web.

• WebAssembly allows native code like Skia to run in browsers.

• GPUs perform the heavy rendering work.

• OpenGL, Vulkan, Metal, WebGL, and DirectX are APIs used to communicate with GPUs.
Understanding these layers makes many Flutter Web discussions much easier to follow and helps explain why Flutter behaves differently across mobile, desktop, and web platforms.
